translation.content: Much of the best electronic music rests along the axes of versatility and curiosity: being able to pull a broad array of influences into your orbit then alchemise it into something fresh and distinctive. That's gyrofield to a tee. Kiana Li began piquing ears in drum & bass as a teenager, releasing records years before setting foot in a club or experiencing the sound on a proper rig. It's a key factor that helps explain how the deeply considered, Hong Kong-born producer arrived at a style that eludes easy description. Li, one of the foremost trans women working in drum & bass today, marshals complex rhythms like a sluice and has a knack for detailed bass programming that floods the stereo field without ever dominating it. You could say her work flutters around the overlap of IDM and breakbeat science, but neither tag fully sticks. 2024's celestial These Heavens was the apotheosis of an already quietly prolific catalogue: we named it the seventh best record of last year, but in truth, anyone who had it as their number one outright would have been on the money too. Li has already followed that spellbinder with a hot run of releases—including a FABRICLIVE 12" this May and a four-tracker on the way for another excellent label; scope the tracklist for a clue—and portal-opening DJ sets. (Those present for their teardown at RA's stage at Horst Arts & Music can attest to that.) True to form, RA.987 is a blur of motion and tactile tones, featuring mainstays (Dillinja, Skee Mask, Calibre, upsammy), contemporary peers (Ehua, Aquarian, Nestax, Maude Vôs), 17 of their own productions and a grip of truly uncommon selections, like Burnt Friedman & João Pais Filipe's "18-140." It's an ambitious mix from one of the most exciting producers at the vanguard of outer-zonal club music right now. – Gabriel Szatan How and where was the mix recorded? Can you tell us the idea behind it? I recorded this mix at a local DJ Studio with three CDJs and a DJM-A9 (with thanks to Sacha and Jochem). I wanted to elaborate upon the selections from gyrofield sets this year, but tailor it to more of a listening experience without losing the fun of it. You've said before that your initial exposure to drum & bass was through "transitive" listening while growing up in Hong Kong, before getting into clubbing down the line. How did that define your ear? Does it carry forward in your productions today? I'd say I listened "transitively" insofar as listening on my cheap headphones and home speakers. I learned to mix and listen critically on my pair of AKGs that I got when I was 14, and actually still use the same pair now! The amazement came from texture and juxtaposition: how we could draw from sounds, instruments, ambiences of so many eras and regions...yet have them make sense in a musical world. The thought of a jungle 808 pounding underneath found sound and '70s funk breaks, brought together with simulated space and echo, felt outlandish on paper but was the backbone of so much music I loved then. The alchemy of it still excites me. Nowadays it's less about the piercing energy of things, I believe you can draw intensity from many ways. You have a purposeful approach to everything you put your name to. Is there a unifying creed that binds your work? Maybe not everyone will pick up on it, but the idea of "inevitability." In my last few records I expressed emotional reactions of things being "the way they are." On my record for Metalheadz it was places and times; in These Heavens it was existential being; in "Flower Burial" it was growing pains and the imperfections of relationships. Ultimately, I hope to feel something from my own work that is beyond description—"inevitability" is just an approximation. I love being surprised by my own work, and imagining ways to upend people's expectations. As well as an ambitious tracklist, this mix has a notable sense of pacing: there are moments—such as the Laurel Halo and upsammy splice—where you draw back when you might be expected to throttle. Is this a way to keep music around 160-180 BPM feeling nimble over a long runtime? I'm really glad you made the observation. I found pacing the most challenging part of this mix. Not playing to a crowd means I had to figure out when to give the audience a break when they listen to this now. That Laurel Halo / upsammy segment isn't entirely out of leftfield: the tracks on either side are also deeper upwellings of energy, and I really wanted to make an ambient section feel like an apex of the music around it. As if the drums peel away to reveal Laurel's voice singing "machine states and empty silhouettes," then milder instrumentation whisks me away on a serenade. I like it because it's poetic. We named These Heavens as one of our favourite records of 2024. You keep up a busy schedule, but that one did seem to connect with a wider audience: were you pleased with the reception? That record has flipped the script and allowed me to embrace music in a way I didn't dare to before. With it I wanted to dodge the pastiche of the genre, hoping it would make a more listenable and fulfilling experience, and with that solidly ticked off for me I'm very glad it got people listening from many corners of electronic music. My fortunes and misfortunes after These Heavens led me to wonderful new connections, and the courage to move forward, to say "that was good, but now I'm going to do something different that is just as good." It induced a seismic shift to my world. What's speaking to you musically right now? Percussive expression, from free jazz to chopped breakbeats to whatever Burnt Friedman is doing. I’m fascinated by different feats of virtuosity, even if it's a computer playing rhythms there's a thrill in things going off the rails, like the sound is unable to contain its emotion. More broadly I feel guided by the paradigms of thinking versus doing. Truthfully I'm in a bit of a creative rut, and find it difficult to pick back up due to personal circumstances. In different schools of world philosophy there are virtues of gaining divine insight, of shifting perception and earnest practice of a craft. Different times call for different measures to light the spark. What are you looking forward to for the rest of 2025? Things have changed so much for me in the past three years I'm almost constantly blindsided. I'm thankful for all the good that's come my way. I feel most excited to make new connections and give what I can for righteous causes.
translation.blurb: Kinetic club energy generated by one of the sharpest talents in drum & bass and beyond.
